content.full_text:
Law Offices of

OLSON, CONNELLY, HAGEL, FONG & LEIDIGH

May 15, 1990

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 "J" Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95814

RE: REQUEST FOR INFORMAL ASSISTANCE

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

This letter is to request informal assistance with
respect to the following question.

QUESTION

A public agency owns land which it desires to have
developed in a manner which furthers its purposes. The
public agency wishes to provide a long-term lease for
the land to any developer who will construct
improvements on the property and operate the
improvements in the manner directed by the land-owner.

In order to attract applicants, the public entity
offers the land at a nominal lease rent. In return,
applicants must agree to rent the improvements only to
qualified renters and at controlled (below-market)
rents. In addition, applicants must construct all
improvements at the applicant's cost and to the
landlord's specifications. Furthermore, at the end of
the long-term tenancy, the improvements become the
property of the public agency land-owner. No money
flows from the public agency to the eventually selected
developer-tenant. The only income received by the
developer-tenant is from the qualified renters who rent
the improvements.

Under the foregoing circumstances, am I correct in
my analysis that the land-owner public entity is not a
"source of income" under the Political Reform Act to
the developer-tenant. In the instant situation, the
applicant, if successful would be providing full
consideration for the lowered leasehold cost of the
land by constructing the improvements and renting them
to only qualified renters at controlled rents.

LANCE H. OLSON
BRUCE J. HAGEL
LEROY N. FONG
ROBERT E. LEIDIGH
GEORGE M. WATERS

OF COUNSEL
LLOYD G. CONNELLY, Member
California State Legislature

300 Capitol Mall, Suite 350
Sacramento, California 95814

TELEPHONE: (916) 442-2952
FAX: (916) 442-1280

Scott Hallabrin
May 15, 1990
Page 2

DISCUSSION

To my knowledge, generally the Commission has not concluded
that in such a situation the person being paid is a source of
income to the person doing the paying.  Thus, even though an
employee provides consideration for his or her pay, he or she is
not viewed as a source of income to the employer. Likewise,
tenants are sources of income to landlords, but not vice versa.
Similarly, a client is a source of income to a lawyer or
accountant, but the lawyer or accountant is not a source of
income to the client merely by providing consideration in return
for the payments by the client. (See Dorn Advice Letter, No. A-
83-086, at page 3.) However, in at least one instance, the
advice was different where a school district employee was
receiving subsidized rent from her employer. (See Wilson Advice
Letter, No. A-81-036.)

Consequently, I ask for your informal assistance in
resolving this question.  Your staff's timely consideration of
this matter will be greatly appreciated.

Very truly yours,

OLSON, CONNELLY, HAGEL, FONG & LEIDIGH

[signature]
ROBERT E. LEIDIGH

REL/kl
